context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about colonialism and development. I’m kind of stuck on how to connect institutional legacies, representations, and technologies of control to the economic development part.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — those connections are really the heart of the prompt. Let's build from what you already know. When you think about colonialism, what's one concrete thing colonial powers actually *did* that shaped the institutions of the countries they controlled?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and needs to begin thinking through the core concepts before writing. Rather than scaffolding all three threads at once, I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them to articulate what they already know. This respects the guided discovery principle and keeps my response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think they set up administrative systems and laws, but I’m not totally sure how specific I should be. Like, is it enough to say they created centralized bureaucracies mainly to extract resources and control people?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid instinct — extractive bureaucracies are a well-documented legacy. Now push it one step further: if those institutions were designed for extraction rather than, say, broad-based development, what might that mean for the countries that inherited them after independence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good observation about extractive institutions. I should validate the direction without giving more content, and push them to think about the connection between that observation and the long-term economic development outcomes — which is what the prompt is really asking about. One targeted question will move them forward.

exchanges.student: I guess it means the state might stay top-down and not very accountable to the public. And maybe the economy could stay organized around exports or extraction instead of building institutions that support wider development?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're starting to draw the link between institutional legacies and economic outcomes. Now, the prompt also asks about 'forms of representation' and 'technologies of control.' What do you think those terms might refer to in a colonial context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good connections between institutional design and economic outcomes. This is solid progress. I should affirm the direction and now push them toward the second part of the prompt — representations and technologies of control — which they haven't addressed yet. This keeps the conversation moving toward a complete response.

exchanges.student: I’m a little fuzzy on those. “Forms of representation” might mean how colonized people were described or categorized, and “technologies of control” maybe means things like censuses, maps, ID systems, policing?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both. Now here's the key question: how do those tools — categorizing people, mapping territory, policing — connect to the economic outcomes you described earlier? What work did they do for the colonial project?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both concepts. I should affirm this and push them to connect these tools to the economic development question, which is the core of the prompt. This keeps the conversation moving toward synthesis rather than dwelling on definitions.

exchanges.student: I think they made extraction and rule more efficient? Like if the colonial state could classify populations and control territory, it could tax people, manage labor, and suppress resistance more easily. I’m just not sure how to phrase that without sounding too obvious.
exchanges.tutor: The substance of your reasoning is strong — don't worry about phrasing for now. Here's what I'd push you on next: is the relationship between colonial experience and economic development the same for all colonized countries? Or does it vary, and if so, why might that be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making the right analytical connections but is now worried about how to phrase things — this edges toward asking me to help with writing, which I should not do. I should redirect them to focus on the intellectual substance rather than the writing, and push them toward the last major component of the prompt: the correlation between colonial experience and economic development outcomes today.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it’s the same for all cases. It probably varies by the type of colonial rule, how long it lasted, whether institutions were mainly extractive or had some investment in infrastructure/education, and what happened after independence.
exchanges.tutor: That's a nuanced and well-reasoned point. You've now worked through all the main threads of the prompt. At this stage, rather than continuing to discuss in the abstract, I'd encourage you to try drafting a paragraph or two that pulls these ideas together. What would your opening argument be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good analytical nuance about variation in colonial outcomes. This is excellent progress. We've now covered all three threads of the prompt. Given that we've touched on institutional legacies, representations, technologies of control, and economic development variation across 5 exchanges, this is a good moment to ask the student to start integrating these ideas into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss in the abstract.

exchanges.student: Maybe my opening argument is that colonialism didn’t just block development in a general way — it actively shaped the institutions, categories of rule, and control systems that many postcolonial states inherited. And those legacies affected development unevenly, often producing extractive governance and unequal economic structures, but with variation across cases.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — it's specific, it accounts for variation, and it ties together the institutional, representational, and control dimensions. That's your opening argument. Now start drafting — get it down in your own words, even if it's rough. You can share what you write and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong thesis that captures the main threads of the prompt. I should affirm this clearly and push them to start writing rather than continuing to refine the argument in conversation. This is the moment to transition from discussion to drafting.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try a rough opening: colonialism shaped development by creating institutions that were meant to control territory and extract labor and resources, not to promote inclusive growth. It also relied on representations of colonized people as backward or governable, plus tools like mapping, census-taking, and policing, which made domination and economic extraction easier. Does that sound like I’m actually answering the prompt, or am I still being too general?
exchanges.tutor: You're genuinely answering the prompt — the three threads are there. But notice what's missing: you discussed earlier how these legacies affected countries *unevenly*. That nuance isn't in your opening yet. How might you bring that in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft opening that addresses the prompt's key elements. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading or writing for them. The opening is good but could be pushed to be more specific about the legacy angle and the variation in outcomes — which they discussed earlier but haven't yet integrated into the draft. I should point this out concisely.

exchanges.student: Maybe I could add that the economic effects weren’t uniform, because colonial systems differed across places and post-independence governments inherited different capacities and constraints. I’m not sure if that makes the thesis too broad though.
exchanges.tutor: Acknowledging variation actually *strengthens* your argument — it shows analytical maturity. Stop deliberating and try adding that sentence to your draft. Then share the fuller version so we can assess whether the whole thing is moving in the right direction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is second-guessing a good instinct. The variation point actually sharpens rather than broadens the thesis. I should push back gently on their self-doubt and encourage them to integrate it into the draft rather than deliberating further. We're at exchange 9 and should be moving toward a more complete written attempt.

exchanges.student: Okay, here’s the fuller version: colonialism shaped development by creating institutions that were meant to control territory and extract labor and resources, not to promote inclusive growth. It also relied on representations of colonized people as backward or governable, plus tools like mapping, census-taking, and policing, which made domination and economic extraction easier. However, the economic effects of colonialism were not identical everywhere, because colonial regimes differed in their goals and methods, and postcolonial states inherited different institutional capacities and constraints.
exchanges.tutor: This is a solid opening — the three threads are present, the variation point is integrated, and the argument is clear. The next challenge is the body of your reflection: you'll need to support these claims. What concrete examples or cases are you thinking of using to illustrate these points?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a well-rounded opening paragraph that addresses all three threads of the prompt and includes the variation point. This is good progress. I should give honest, constructive feedback and now push them toward the rest of the paper — they have 600+ words still to write and need to develop their arguments with examples and evidence. I should not grade but can evaluate generally.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. At no point did the tutor supply the thesis, evidence, or final wording; instead, each turn posed a targeted question that drew the next idea from the student. Scaffolding was progressive and responsive, building directly on each student contribution. Responses were concise and well-calibrated to the student's level. No off-topic drift, no grading language, and no redundancy were observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a near-submission-ready answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key conclusion before the student reached it. Every substantive idea — extractive bureaucracies, representational categories, technologies of control, variation across cases — was elicited from the student through questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming 'solid instinct,' 'nuanced and well-reasoned point') and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., asking what the student already knew before explaining). 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, notably pointing out that the variation nuance was missing from the draft and explaining why including it strengthens the argument. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation advanced steadily without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the prompt throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were brief, focused, and supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
